claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 17 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 17 and in approved education
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child is not in local authority care
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 17 and in approved education; Child lives with claimant; Child is not in local authority care; Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child is 17 and in approved education
Child is not in local authority care
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: I'll help you determine if you're eligible for Child Benefit. Let me start the assessment process.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! To start, I need to understand a bit about your child (or children).

**First question: How old is your child?**

This is important because eligibility depends on age:
- Children **under 16** may automatically qualify
- Children **aged 16-19** may qualify if they're in approved education or training
- Children **aged 20 or over** are generally not eligible

Please tell me:
1. How many children do you want to check eligibility for?
2. What is/are their age(s)?
3. What are their names (so I can keep track of each child)?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hi, thanks for helping me with this. I have one child – my son Alex. He's 17 years old and lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! So we're assessing eligibility for your 17-year-old son, Alex.

Since Alex is 17 (which falls in the 16-19 age category), his eligibility will depend on whether he's in approved education or training. Let me navigate to the next step.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect. Now I need to know about Alex's current education or training situation.

**Is Alex currently:**

1. **In approved education or training** (such as full-time non-advanced education at school, college, or approved training), OR
2. **Recently left education** (within the last 20 weeks), OR  
3. **Neither** - not in education/training and didn't recently leave?

Please let me know which situation applies to Alex.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex is still in education. He's studying for his A-levels at the local sixth form college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Alex is in education studying for his A-levels. Now I need to check that the education level meets the requirements for Child Benefit. Let me move to the next step.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great news! A-levels are specifically listed as **approved (non-advanced) education** for Child Benefit purposes. Now I need to verify a few more details about Alex's education:

**Questions about Alex's A-level studies:**

1. **Is this full-time education?** (At least 12 hours per week of supervised study)
2. **Is this education paid for or arranged by his employer?** (or does he have a guaranteed job with them after finishing?)
3. **Is Alex married or in a civil partnership?** (If yes, we'd need to check if he's financially dependent on you)

Please let me know the answers to these questions.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text:
Yes, it's full-time education – he's at college during the week like any normal sixth form student. 

No, it's not paid for by an employer or anything like that. He's just doing his A-levels at the local college.

And no, he's not married.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Perfect! Alex meets all the criteria for approved education:
- ✓ A-levels (approved non-advanced qualification)
- ✓ Full-time (12+ hours per week)
- ✓ Not employer-funded
- ✓ Not married

Let me move to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check Alex's work situation.

**Does Alex work less than 24 hours per week** (or is he not in paid work at all)?

For context: Children aged 16+ who work 24 hours or more per week are not eligible for Child Benefit, even if they're in education.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he doesn't work at all. He's just focused on his studies at the moment.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! Alex is not working, so that's fine. Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Next question about Alex:

**Is Alex receiving any benefits in his own right?** Specifically:
- Employment and Support Allowance (ESA)
- Universal Credit
- Or similar benefits claimed in his own name

If he's **NOT** receiving any of these benefits, that's fine and we can continue.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, he's not receiving any benefits in his own name. He's just a student living at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Alex is not receiving any benefits in his own right. Let me move to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to check about apprenticeships.

**Is Alex in an apprenticeship?**

For your information:
- Most apprenticeships (English apprenticeships, Scottish Modern Apprenticeships, most Welsh apprenticeships except Foundation level) would disqualify him
- But you mentioned he's doing A-levels at sixth form college, so I assume he's **not** in an apprenticeship?

Please confirm whether Alex is or isn't in an apprenticeship.

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: That's right, he's not in an apprenticeship. He's just doing his A-levels at college.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! Alex is not in an apprenticeship. Now we've confirmed Alex qualifies as an eligible child, let me move on to check your eligibility as the claimant.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Excellent! Now I need to check your eligibility as the claimant. The first requirement is about residency.

**Do you meet the UK residency requirements?**

You can qualify if **ANY** of the following applies to you:

1. **You normally live in the UK**
2. You have settled status under the EU Settlement Scheme
3. You have pre-settled status and are working (earning above the National Insurance threshold)
4. You have pre-settled status and are actively looking for work
5. You have pre-settled status with sufficient resources to support yourself
6. You have pre-settled status and are studying with sufficient resources
7. You're a family member of an EEA/Swiss national with right to reside
8. You're a Crown servant living abroad (or in certain qualifying countries)

Do any of these apply to you? Most commonly, people simply normally live in the UK - is that your situation?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right. I normally live in the UK – we're based here in England.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! You meet the residency requirements as you normally live in the UK. Let me move to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great! Now I need to confirm you're responsible for Alex.

**Are you responsible for Alex?**

You can qualify if **ANY** of these applies:

1. **You live with Alex** (which you've already mentioned he lives at home with you)
2. You contribute to Alex's upkeep (paying at least the same amount as Child Benefit towards their care - food, clothes, pocket money, housing, etc.)
3. You pay maintenance under a court order or agreement

Based on what you've told me, Alex lives at home with you, so you meet this requirement. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's correct. Alex lives at home with me.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent! You're responsible for Alex as he lives with you. Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check about other potential claimants.

**Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?**

For example:
- Alex's other parent
- Another family member
- Anyone else who might be claiming

This is important because only one person can claim Child Benefit for the same child at any given time.

What's your situation regarding this?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex. It's just me looking after him.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Perfect! No one else is claiming for Alex. Let me move to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Now I need to check Alex's living situation to see if any special rules apply.

**Which of the following describes Alex's current living situation?**

1. **Living normally at home with you** (no special circumstances)
2. In hospital (for an extended period)
3. In local authority care
4. In a fostering arrangement

Based on what you've told me, it sounds like Alex is living normally at home with you. Is that correct?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, that's right. Alex just lives at home with me – nothing unusual about his living situation.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Great! Alex is living normally at home. Let me continue to the next check.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Just one final check:

**Is the local council paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance?**

In other words, is the council contributing financially towards Alex's living costs or upkeep?

If the answer is **no** (the council is NOT paying), then you should be eligible!

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything towards Alex. I'm the one looking after him and covering all his costs.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text: Excellent news! Let me navigate to the final outcome.

conversation.author: child_benefit_eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
## 🎉 Great News - You ARE Eligible for Child Benefit!

Based on the information you've provided, **you meet all the eligibility requirements** for Child Benefit for your son Alex.

### Why You're Eligible:

**Alex qualifies as an eligible child because:**
- ✓ He's 17 years old (in the 16-19 age category)
- ✓ He's in approved non-advanced education (A-levels at sixth form college)
- ✓ His education is full-time (12+ hours per week)
- ✓ His education is not employer-funded
- ✓ He's not married
- ✓ He's not working 24+ hours per week
- ✓ He's not receiving benefits in his own right
- ✓ He's not in a disqualifying apprenticeship

**You qualify as the claimant because:**
- ✓ You normally live in the UK
- ✓ You're responsible for Alex (he lives at home with you)
- ✓ No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex
- ✓ Alex lives normally at home
- ✓ The local council is not paying towards Alex's care

### Next Steps to Claim:

1. **Apply online or by post** using form CH2
2. **Provide proof of identity** for yourself and Alex
3. **Provide Alex's birth certificate** or adoption certificate
4. **Be aware**: If you or your partner earns over £60,000, you may need to pay the High Income Child Benefit Charge (but you can still claim)
5. **Payments**: Made every 4 weeks (or weekly if you're a single parent or receiving certain benefits)

### How to Apply:
Visit: https://www.gov.uk/child-benefit/how-to-claim

Congratulations! If you have any questions about the application process, feel free to ask.

reasonings: Alex is eligible as a qualifying child because: (1) He is 17 years old, which falls in the 16-19 age category; (2) He is in approved non-advanced education (studying A-levels at sixth form college); (3) His education is full-time (12+ hours per week of supervised study); (4) His education is not employer-funded; (5) He is not married; (6) He is not working 24+ hours per week; (7) He is not receiving benefits in his own right (such as ESA or Universal Credit); (8) He is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. The claimant is eligible because: (1) They normally live in the UK (meeting residency requirements); (2) They are responsible for Alex as he lives at home with them; (3) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (4) Alex lives normally at home (not in hospital, care, or fostering); (5) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all eligibility requirements for Child Benefit for their 17-year-old son Alex, who is in full-time approved education (A-levels) and lives at home with the claimant in the UK.

response.description: Determine child's age category for eligibility
response.routing_logic: Under 16: automatic qualification. 16-19: check education/training. 20+: not eligible

response.description: For 16-19 year olds, check if in education/training or eligible for extension
response.routing_logic: If in education/training, validate type and level. If just left education (within 20 weeks), check extension eligibility.

response.question: Is the child in approved (non-advanced) education or training?
response.help_text: Approved education: A-levels, T-levels, NVQ Level 3 or below, Scottish Highers/Advanced Highers. Advanced (NOT approved): University degrees, HNC, HND, NVQ Level 4+. Must be full-time (12+ hours/week supervised study).

criteria.must_not_be_married_unless_dependent: Married 16-19 year olds only qualify if financially dependent on claimant

response.question: Is the child working less than 24 hours per week (or not in paid work)?
response.help_text: Children aged 16+ working 24+ hours per week and not in approved education/training are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT receiving certain benefits in their own right (e.g., ESA, Universal Credit)?
response.help_text: Children receiving Employment and Support Allowance or Universal Credit in their own right are not eligible

response.question: Is the child NOT in a disqualifying apprenticeship?
response.help_text: Regional rules apply: English apprenticeships (all types) disqualify. Scottish Modern Apprenticeships disqualify. Welsh Foundation Apprenticeships are APPROVED. Other Welsh apprenticeships disqualify.

response.question: Does the claimant meet UK residency requirements?
response.help_text: Must normally live in the UK or meet special circumstances

paths.requirement: Claimant normally lives in the UK

paths.description: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
paths.requirement: Has settled status under EU Settlement Scheme

criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Working and earning/expect to earn above primary National Insurance threshold for 3 continuous months

paths.description: Pre-settled status and actively seeking work
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Looking for work (can claim as jobseeker for up to 91 days)
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Has sufficient resources to support self and family financially
NOT claiming Income Support, income-based JSA, Pension Credit, or Universal Credit
Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Studying and has sufficient resources to support self financially

paths.description: Pre-settled status as family member of EEA/Swiss national
criteria: Has pre-settled status under EU Settlement Scheme
Is family member of EEA or Swiss national with right to reside
One of: spouse/civil partner, dependent child/grandchild under 21, dependent parent/grandparent

paths.requirement: Living abroad as Crown servant or in certain qualifying countries

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to responsibility check

response.question: Are you responsible for the child?
response.help_text: Responsibility means living with child OR contributing to their upkeep

criteria: You pay at least the same amount as Child Benefit (or equivalent in kind) towards their care
Contributions include: food, clothes, pocket money, housing, presents
Can be weekly, monthly, or lump sum payments

paths.description: Paying maintenance under court order or agreement
criteria: Payments made towards child's maintenance under court order or agreement
Amount equals or exceeds Child Benefit payment

response.evaluation_logic: If ANY path is satisfied, proceed to child location checks

response.description: Check if another person is claiming Child Benefit for this child
response.routing_logic: If no other claimant, proceed. If other claimant exists and parties agree who should claim, route accordingly. If dispute exists, defer to HMRC for arbitration.

response.description: Determine if special rules apply based on child's location
response.routing_logic: Check if child is in hospital, care, fostered, or living normally

response.question: Is the local council NOT paying towards the child's accommodation or maintenance?
response.help_text: You generally cannot get Child Benefit if the local council is paying for the child

response.description: Applicant meets all criteria for Child Benefit
response.reason: Meets all Child Benefit eligibility requirements
next_steps: Apply online or by post using form CH2
Provide proof of identity for yourself and the child
Provide child's birth certificate or adoption certificate
Be aware of High Income Child Benefit Charge if you or partner earns over £60,000
Payments made every 4 weeks (or weekly if single parent or receiving certain benefits)

child_evaluations.reasoning: Alex is eligible as a qualifying child because: (1) He is 17 years old, which falls in the 16-19 age category; (2) He is in approved non-advanced education (studying A-levels at sixth form college); (3) His education is full-time (12+ hours per week of supervised study); (4) His education is not employer-funded; (5) He is not married; (6) He is not working 24+ hours per week; (7) He is not receiving benefits in his own right (such as ESA or Universal Credit); (8) He is not in a disqualifying apprenticeship. The claimant is eligible because: (1) They normally live in the UK (meeting residency requirements); (2) They are responsible for Alex as he lives at home with them; (3) No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex; (4) Alex lives normally at home (not in hospital, care, or fostering); (5) The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

response.overall_reasoning: The claimant meets all eligibility requirements for Child Benefit for their 17-year-old son Alex, who is in full-time approved education (A-levels) and lives at home with the claimant in the UK.
